title: Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread Loaf

seo.title: Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread Recipe | Fluffy & Soft Loaf
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make the ultimate Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread! This recipe yields an incredibly fluffy, soft, and delicious loaf perfect for sandwiches or toast.

seo.imageAlt: A freshly baked, golden brown Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread loaf on a wooden cutting board.
seo.imageCaption: Bake the perfect Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread at home with our easy-to-follow recipe.

description: Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread is renowned for its incredibly soft, fluffy texture and delicate sweetness, making it a beloved staple in Japanese households. This recipe utilizes the tangzhong method, a traditional technique that ensures an exceptionally moist and long-lasting loaf. Perfect for sandwiches, toast, or simply enjoyed plain, this homemade milk bread will elevate your everyday baking.

instructions.title: Prepare the Tangzhong
instructions.text: In a small saucepan, whisk together 30g water, 30g milk, and 20g bread flour until smooth. Heat over medium-low heat, stirring constantly with a whisk, until the mixture thickens to a paste-like consistency, resembling wallpaper paste. This should take about 2-3 minutes and reach 65C (149F). Remove from heat, transfer to a small bowl, cover with plastic wrap directly on the surface to prevent a skin from forming, and let it cool completely to room temperature (about 15-20 minutes).

instructions.title: Combine Dry Ingredients
instructions.text: In the bowl of a stand mixer fitted with the dough hook attachment, combine 250g bread flour, 30g granulated sugar, 3g salt, and 4g instant dry yeast. Mix on low speed for 30 seconds to distribute the ingredients evenly.

instructions.title: Mix Wet Ingredients and Dough
instructions.text: Add 100g lukewarm whole milk, the large egg, and the cooled tangzhong to the dry ingredients in the mixer. Mix on low speed until a shaggy dough forms, scraping down the sides of the bowl as needed. Continue mixing for about 5-7 minutes until the dough comes together and starts to look cohesive.

instructions.title: Knead the Dough and Add Butter
instructions.text: Increase the mixer speed to medium-low and knead for another 5 minutes. The dough should start to pull away from the sides of the bowl. Add the 30g softened unsalted butter, a little at a time, until fully incorporated. Continue kneading on medium speed for 10-15 minutes, or until the dough is very smooth, elastic, and passes the 'windowpane test' (a small piece of dough can be stretched thinly enough to see light through it without tearing).

instructions.title: First Proof (Bulk Fermentation)
instructions.text: Form the dough into a smooth ball and place it in a lightly oiled large bowl. Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap or a damp towel and let it rise in a warm place for 60-90 minutes, or until doubled in size. The exact time will depend on the temperature of your kitchen.

instructions.title: Shape the Loaf
instructions.text: Gently punch down the risen dough to release the air. Transfer it to a lightly floured surface. Divide the dough into three equal pieces. Roll each piece into a rectangle (approximately 8x4 inches). Fold the longer sides of the rectangle towards the center, then roll the dough tightly from one short end to the other to form a log. Place the three logs side by side in a greased 9x5 inch loaf pan, seam-side down.

instructions.title: Second Proof (Final Rise)
instructions.text: Cover the loaf pan loosely with plastic wrap or a damp towel and let it rise in a warm place for 45-60 minutes, or until the dough has risen about 1 inch above the rim of the pan. Towards the end of the proofing time, preheat your oven to 190C (375F).

instructions.title: Bake the Shokupan
instructions.text: Once the dough has risen, bake in the preheated oven for 30-35 minutes, or until the top is golden brown and the internal temperature reaches 93-96C (200-205F) when checked with an instant-read thermometer. If the top browns too quickly, you can loosely tent it with aluminum foil.

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the loaf from the oven and immediately invert it onto a wire rack to cool completely. This prevents the bottom from becoming soggy. Slice the Shokupan only when it has fully cooled to room temperature for the best texture. Enjoy plain, toasted, or as a base for your favorite sandwiches.

tips: Ensure your tangzhong cools completely before adding it to the main dough to prevent prematurely activating the yeast. Warm tangzhong can speed up fermentation too much.
Achieving a strong windowpane test is crucial for soft, airy Shokupan. If kneading by hand, this may take 20-30 minutes of consistent work.

For best results, allow the Shokupan to cool completely before slicing. Slicing warm bread can compress the delicate crumb and make it gummy. Store leftovers in an airtight bag at room temperature for up to 3 days, or freeze for longer storage.
article: Shokupan Japanese Milk Bread Loaf is a beloved Japanese Baking dish enjoyed across Japanese. This authentic recipe brings traditional flavors to your kitchen.
